Navigating unfamiliar environments is a significant challenge for
blind and low-vision (BLV) individuals, both indoors and outdoors.
While traditional mobility aids such as white canes and guide dogs
provide essential support, they have limitations in detecting ob-
stacles and identifying clear paths in complex environments. To
.
enhance mobility, BLV individuals rely on assistive technologies
t
such as GPS-based navifgation systems, AI-powered smart glasses
[20], and sonar-equipaped smart canes [9]. These solutions provide
audio or haptic feerdback for obstacle detection and route guidance.
However, despdite these advancements, existing technologies still
face limitations in real-time navigation, precise obstacle avoidance,
and envirgonmental aware.ness, particularly in dynamic and unfa-
miliarnscenarios. n
Miany GPS-based assistive technologies, including Be My Eyes
o
k[12], Seeing AI [1i0], and Aira [1], offer voice guidance and real-time
rassistance. Hotwever, they often struggle with providing precise
localizationu, real-time obstacle avoidance, and effective pathfinding
in unstrbuctured environments. Additionally, AI-powered solutions,
suchias computer vision-based smart glasses [2], require high com-
r
putational power, making them costly and less accessible for many
t
susers. Given these challenges, there is a need for a more efficient,
low-cost, and user-friendly navigation solution that leverages image
processing and depth estimation techniques to assist BLV individu-
als effectively.
In this paper, we examine various navigation techniques, in-
cluding those that use Vision Language Models (VLMs), and then
present a computer vision-based algorithm for recognizing free
space and enabling pathfinding. The systemalso incorporatesmonoc-
ular depth estimation to generate depth maps, which, when pro-
cessed using LLMs, improve obstacle detection and free path identi-
fication. Finally, we propose a novel image-processing approach that
leverages Depth-First Search (DFS) on depth images to identify the
longest free path, ensuring optimal route selection for BLV individ-
uals. This computationally efficient technique enhances real-time
navigation by providing accurate obstacle-free paths while main-
taining low processing costs. Compared to existing AI-powered
solutions, our method offers a user-friendly, computationally effi-
cient, and cost-effective alternative without compromising accuracy
or responsiveness, making it a practical and accessible solution for
BLV individuals to navigate safely and independently.
To evaluate the effectiveness of our system, we aim to answer
the following research questions:
• RQ1: How useful is our proposed navigation scheme for
BLV people?
• RQ2: How effective are AI-powered solutions for real-time
navigation compared to our method?

• RQ3: How well does our method address the shortcomings
of AI-powered solutions for real-time navigation?
• RQ4: How well does the implemented system address BLV
individuals’ needs, and what are their impressions of its
effectiveness?
To answer RQ1, we conducted preliminary studies on BLV in-
dividuals’ navigation habits and identified key challenges, such as
difficulties in anticipating environmental layouts, avoiding obsta-
cles, and maintaining a straight path in dynamic settings. Our ap-
proach aims to overcome these challenges through a cost-effective,
real-time navigation aid that can be used with a single-camera
smartphone.
To answer RQ2, we compare ourmethodwith existingAI-powered
solutions that rely on high-end computational models for naviga-
tion. While AI-based systems provide valuable insights, they often
require significant resources and may not perform optimally in all
environments. Our approach, utilizing depth maps and DFS-based
free-path detection, offers a lightweight yet efficient alternative.
To answer RQ3, we analyze the primary limitations of current
AI-powered navigation solutions, including high computational
costs, reliance on expensive hardware, and potential inaccuracies
in real-time obstacle detection. Our method addresses these issues
by using a more accessible and computationally efficient approach,
ensuring better accuracy, faster processing, and enhanced usability.
To answer RQ4, we are conducting user studies and surveys
in February 2025 to evaluate how well the implemented system
o
addresses BLV individuals’ needs and their impressions of its ef-
fectiveness. This evaluation aligns with ongoing advancemwents
in assistive technology, urban infrastructure developments, and
relevant policy changes. Early surveys assessing publicdinterest in
assistive navigation solutions have highlighted key conecerns regard-
i
ing privacy, data security, and long-term usabilihty [8]. Our study
d
considers these factors to refine the system,senhance user trust,
and improve adoption, ensuring it effectivelyimeets the real-wrorld
l
navigation challenges faced by BLV individuals.  o
b
While our system offers significant advantages in termfs of acces-
sibility, efficiency, and accuracy, weuacknowledge potential limita-
t
tions related to privacy concernspand adaptability in highly dynamic
o
environments. Our researchnadds to the larger goal of increasing
accessibility and independence by creatinNg a user-friendly, cost-
effective, and efficientUnavigation system for BLV persons. Fur-
thermore, the approaches investigated in this study can contribute
to future advancements in real-time assistive technology for peo-
ple with visual impairments, as well as inspire breakthroughs in
autonomous navigation systems for personal and public use.
2  RELATEDWORKS
Developing successful navigation systems for blind and low-vision
(BLV) people has been a major focus of assistive technology re-
search. Various methodologies have been investigated, ranging
from GPS-based solutions and depth-sensing techniques to AI-
powered systems and infrastructure-free navigation tools. While
many of these technologies strive to increase mobility, obstacle
detection, and spatial awareness, they frequently confront hurdles
in real-time processing, environmental adaptability, and user acces-
sibility. This section examines existing research on BLV navigation

Das et al.
systems, depth estimate methods, and AI-powered assistive tech-
nologies, emphasizing their contributions, limits, and implications
for the development of our proposed solution. Table 1 highlights
significant studies on BLV navigation and accessibility, including
techniques, conclusions, and limitations.
2.1  Navigation Systems for BLV Individuals
Navigating unknown regions poses significant obstacles for blind
and low-vision (BLV) people, demanding intensive study into as-
sisted navigation technologies. A variety of approaches have been
investigated, ranging from GPS-based solutions to sensor-driven
indoor navigation systems.
Traditional GPS-based technology have been important in assist-
ing BLV patients. Microsoft Soundscape [14], for example, improves
.
spatial awareness with 3D audio cues. Liu et al. [7] studied user in-
t
teractionwith Soundscapfe and discovered that spatial audio callouts
considerably improvead usability and retention. Their investigation
did, however, iderntify limits in exact localization and real-time
obstacle avoiddance, both of which are significant challenges in
GPS-based systems.
To addgress the limitati.ons of GPS-based technology, other so-
lutionns based on ambinent sensors and computer vision have been
inveistigated. Jain et al. [5] describes a system that uses current
o
kstreet cameras toideliver real-time aural feedback to BLV pedestri-
r t
ans. By using computer vision algorithms to street camera feeds, the
system prouvides users with detailed spatial information that aids
in obstabcle avoidance and safe street crossing.

While this strategy
i
improves real-time navigation, its reliance on public monitoring
r
infrastructure limits its scalability.
t
sFor indoor navigation, Tsai et al. [18] proposed inertial-based
iOS applications—Wayfinding and Backtracking—that use a smart-
phone’s internal sensors for pathfindingwithout relying on external
infrastructure like Bluetooth beacons. The Wayfinding software
enables hands-free navigation along established routes, while Back-
tracking allows users to retrace their steps in unfamiliar areas.
Although these systems are useful, they suffer from localization
drift—a progressive rise in positioning errors over time—which
leads to lower navigation accuracy, especially in bigger or feature-
less areas.
Depth sensing combined into portable devices has also emerged
as a viable solution. See et al. [15] created a smartphone application
that uses depth cameras to identify obstacles and recognize objects.
This infrastructure-free technique provides real-time environmen-
tal awareness via a mobile device. In controlled experiments, the
system detected obstacles accurately, although performance varied
depending on environmental conditions such as lighting and item
size.
Robotic help expands the capabilities of navigation aids. Kurib-
ayashi et al. [6] built a suitcase-shaped robot (PathFinder) that helps
blind people navigate unfamiliar indoor places by dynamically rec-
ognizing junctions and reading signs. The solution gives real-time
input without relying on prebuilt maps, giving users more flexibility.
However, it needs users to recall routes while navigating, which
might raise cognitive burden and impair usefulness.
Ren et al. [13] proposed RouteNav, which is a hybrid system that
combines GPS localization and inertial dead reckoning to increase
2025-04-30 01:06. Page 2 of 1–14.

Real-Time Wayfinding Assistant for Blind and Low-Vision Users
navigation accuracy in complex areas like transportation hubs.
This system mitigates the impact of GPS inaccuracy by segmenting
environments into a tile-based navigation system that provides
multimodal feedback such as voice, haptics, and aural signals. While
the system is excellent in organized surroundings, it still faces issues
with localization drift and GPS signal discrepancies, particularly in
underground or open locations.
2.2  AI-Powered Scene Description and
Accessibility Tools
Beyond navigation, AI-powered scene description tools have played
an important role in increasing BLV users’ environmental aware-
ness. These applications combine computer vision and natural lan-
guage processing to deliver real-time descriptions of the environ-
ment, objects, and people in order to promote user independence.
However, issues such as trust in AI outputs, description accuracy,
and user experience remain major concerns.
Penuela et al. [3] looked into how BLV users engage with AI-
powered scene description apps, including their trust, satisfaction,
and usage habits. The study discovered that users regularly used
these tools for object recognition and scene assessment in familiar
interior settings, based on a two-week diary study with 16 partici-
pants. Interestingly, despite occasional mistakes in AI-generated
descriptions, users frequently relied on the system, demonstrat-
ing a nuanced link between trust and perceived utility. The study
also stressed the significance of increasing AI accuracy and usero
customisation choices in order to improve satisfaction and usability.
w
Another interesting method [15] is to include depth photogra-
phy into assistive applications to improve obstacle identification
d
and spatial awareness [15]. A depth camera-based navigation tool
e
was created to help users recognize barriers and locate things by i
providing aural and haptic input, removing the nheed for extra ex-
d
s
ternal hardware. The technology successfulily supplied real-time
r
input but had limitations in different conltexts. Furthermore, the
o
object detection model was limited to sbpecific categories, such as
f
furniture, entrances, and everydayudomestic things, restricting its
t
applicability in unknown environments.
p
To broaden the scope of AI-powered accessiboility, Seo et al. [16]
explored how BLV users intneract with multimodal large language
N
models (LLMs) to interUpret complicated data visualizations. The
study launched maidrAI, an AI-powered data visualization tool
that combines text, audio, and haptic input, allowing BLV users
to interact with data using organized, customized descriptions.
User research found that participants preferred clear, organized
outputs and valued the opportunity to tailor AI-generated solu-
tions.

However, issues such as AI hallucinations (inaccurate or falsi-
fied information) and the difficulty of rapid engineering remained,
emphasizing the need for more dependable and user-friendly AI
systems.
Efforts to increase data accessibility for BLVusers, ChartA11y[21]
allows blind people to interact with 2D data visualizations via mul-
timodal interfaces on their smartphones. The technology, which
combines touch-based exploration with audio and haptic feedback,
gives users access to scatter plots, bar charts, and line charts. User
tests demonstrated the usefulness of mixing structured navigation
with direct touch interactions, however obstacles such as gesture
2025-04-30 01:06. Page 3 of 1–14.

complexity and small sample sizes in testing indicated areas for
additional research.
2.3  User Engagement and Broader Assistive
Technologies
User-centered design and engagement strategies are crucial for
developing assistive technologies that meet the requirements and
preferences of blind and low-vision (BLV) people. Several studies
have looked into how design decisions affect user adoption, satisfac-
tion, and long-term involvement with navigation and accessibility
technologies.
In their study, Liu et al. [7] examined user behavior using Mi-
crosoft Soundscape to better understand the aspects that influence
long-termengagement. The study found that early user interactions,
particularly those during th.e first week, were major indicators of
t
continuing use. Features such as spatial audio callouts and street
f
previews helped to retain users. However, the study also noted
a
difficulties in extrapolating findings to other navigation tools and
r
advocated for the incorporation of qualitative user feedback to
d
better customize future solutions.
Tsai etgal. [18] highlight the need for flexible, infrastructure-
.
free navigation tools by introducing iOS applications for interior
n n
naviigation using a phone’s built-in inertial sensors. By eliminating
the requirement foroexternal beacons or pre-installed infrastructure,
k i
rthe system entabled hands-free navigation, which is critical for
BLV people who frequently rely on canes or guide dogs. While
u
user studies validated the system’s usefulness, concerns like as
b
localiization drift and decreased accuracy in wide, open settings
hamrpered its general applicability.
tEducational accessibility for BLV students has also received sub-
s
stantial attention in assistive technology research. Shaheen (2024)
[17] investigated the difficulties encountered by BLV students in
K-12 school, particularly in using instructional tools. The study
discovered that many children gained assistive technology (AT)
literacy outside of school due to inadequacies in formal educa-
tion assistance. Complex user interfaces, unlabeled buttons, and
irregular navigation frameworks hampered academic involvement,
particularly in STEM disciplines.
Urban navigation provides new issues, notably in picking the
most relevant information to assist BLV users during mobility ac-
tivities. Hoogsteen et al. [4] explored which environmental charac-
teristics are easiest to describe for blind pedestrians. Intersections,
traffic lights, and crosswalks were judged to be the most useful,
while things such as poles or fire hydrants were deemed less rele-
vant. Interestingly, the study found that early and late blind people
had varied information needs, with late blind users requiring more
extensive environmental descriptions. The study provided a struc-
tured framework for selecting information in assistive navigation
systems, with the goal of lowering cognitive burden and improving
spatial awareness.
2.4  Gaps in Existing Research
Despite major advances in assisted navigation and AI-powered
accessibility technologies, issues such as processing efficiency, real-
time flexibility, usability, and scalability continue to impede their
practical adoption by blind and low-vision (BLV) people..

One significant barrier is the processing cost of AI-powered sys-
tems. Many depth estimation and object identification methods
o
need a lot of computing power, which makes them unsuitable for
mobile deployment. While some technologies solve these rewstric-
tions, relying on specialist hardware like depth cameras and LiDAR
raises costs and reduces accessibility [15]. Although ligdhtweight
AI models have been proposed, they frequently forefeit accuracy,
i
limiting their practical application.
h
d
Adaptability to changing environments is asnother key concern.
Many navigation systems rely on pre-mappeid layouts or prersume
l
static surroundings, rendering them ineffective in real-woorld urban
b
environments with ever-changing impediments. Efforts tfo integrate
external infrastructure, such as publuic surveillance cameras, aim to
t
address this issue, but reliance opn fixed installations limits scalabil-
o
ity [5]. Similarly, inertial-based indoor navigation systems exhibit
n
localization drift over time, resulting in poNsitioning errors [18].
Excessive or incorrecUt information frequently causes confusion
and reduces trust in assistive tools [3]. AI hallucinations, where
generated responses are misleading or overly detailed, present addi-
tional concerns, particularly in multimodal accessibility tools [16].
These inconsistencies diminish the reliability of AI-based assis-
tance, making it difficult for users to rely on such systems in critical
situations.
Customization and user flexibility are frequently ignored. Nu-
merous assistive solutions adopt a uniform approach, neglecting
the varied needs of users. Although multimodal interfaces have
enhanced accessibility for data visualization, they often fall short in
providing customization for various degrees of visual impairment
[21]. Furthermore, studies emphasize the differing information pref-
erences of people who are blind from birth compared to those who
lost their sight later, but many navigation technologies do not ac-
commodate these differences [4].

3 METHODOLOGY
This section describes the navigation approaches explored in this
study, including different pathfinding schemes, AI-powered scene
description, and our proposed image-processing-based technique
for BLV individuals’ real-time navigation.
3.1  Navigation Schemes
To guide BLV individuals effectively, we consider multiple navi-
gation schemes that define movement direction and obstacle-free
pathfinding:
3.1.1  Left/Forward/Right (DOF 3). This approach provides three
degrees of freedom (DOF), allowing users to navigate in left, for-
ward, or right directions based on obstacle-free regions. It simplifies
decision-making but may lack precision in complex environments.
3.1.2  In Degree (DOF 180). A more granular approach, where the
free path is determined across a 180-degree field of view, offering
finer directional control for more precise navigation.
3.1.3  Clock Hour Hand (DOF 13).

Inspired by the clock-face repre-
sentation, this method divides the navigation space into 13 distinct
directions, mimicking how blind individuals often conceptualize
orientation (e.g., "move toward 12 o’clock" for straight ahead). This
approach balances accuracy and user intuitiveness, making it a
practical solution for real-world applications.

3.2  AI-Assisted Navigation Approaches
This study explores six different navigation approaches, five of
which leverage AI models for obstacle avoidance and pathfinding.
In contrast, the proposed Approach 6 introduces a computation-
ally efficient image processing algorithm using Depth-First Search
(DFS) to determine the longest free path. These approaches vary in
input data, processing techniques, and model complexity, aiming
to balance accuracy, efficiency, and real-time usability.
3.2.1  Approach 1: 2DRGB Image to Vision LanguageModels (VLMs).
In this approach, we use 2D RGB images with a resolution of 64×64
pixels to simplify processing, reduce computational complexity,
2025-04-30 01:06. Page 5 of 1–14.

and optimize inference time. The images are then passed to Vision
Language Models (VLMs), which analyze the scene and infer the
best path for navigation based on visual features. While this method
benefits fromVLMs’ reasoning capabilities, its reliance on RGB data
limits depth perception and precise obstacle avoidance.
3.2.2  Approach 2: Depth Images to VLMs. 64×64 depth images are
used instead of RGB images to enhance depth perception. Depth
information allows VLMs to understand spatial relationships more
effectively, improving obstacle detection and navigation decisions.
VLMs still require significant computational power for real-time
processing, making this approach less efficient for low-resource
devices.
3.2.3  Approach 3: Combined RGB and Depth Images to VLMs. This
approach combines 64×64 RGB and depth images, providing VLMs
.
with visual and depth infortmation for pathfinding. The model gains
a richer understandingfof the environment by fusing these two
modalities, leading tao improved accuracy. Consequently, the in-
r
creased input complexity results in higher computational costs,
making real-timde implementation more challenging.
3.2.4  Apgproach 4: Patch-B.ased Depth Representation to LLMs. In-
steadnof using full-resolution depth images, this method processes
n
depith data in a 5×5 grid of patch values, significantly reducing
o
kinput size whileiretaining essential spatial details. The processed
rdepth patchestare fed into LLMs, which analyze the segmented
depth infourmation for navigation. Although this method lowers
computbational requirements, the reduction in resolution may im-
pact aiccuracy in complex environments.
r
3t.2.5  Approach 5: Classification-Based Pathfinding using Vision
s
Transformers (ViT). This approach formulates pathfinding as a clas-
sification task, where a Vision Transformer (ViT) processes images
to predict the best navigation direction. ViTs are powerful deep-
learning models that capture long-range dependencies within an
image. To enable this, we manually annotated a dataset with direc-
tional labels and trained a ViT model to learn the mapping between
image features and navigation decisions.

However, ViTs require
extensive computational resources and large-scale training data,
limiting their feasibility for real-time deployment in assistive navi-
gation applications.
3.3  Approach 6: DFS-Based Pathfinding from
Patched Depth Images (Proposed)
While AI-assisted solutions have demonstrated strong performance
in complex perception tasks, they often suffer from significant
limitations in real-world applications. These deep learning-based
approaches typically demand substantial computational resources,
rely on high-end hardware, and consume considerable energy—making
them inefficient and unsuitable for real-time or on-device use, par-
ticularly in low-power or embedded systems. Additionally, they
often require large annotated datasets for training and may struggle
to generalize well across diverse, unseen environments.
To address these challenges, we propose a computationally ef-
ficient image processing algorithm for pathfinding. Our method
leverages depth images obtained from monocular depth estimation
and utilizes a 15×15 patch-based depth representation. Instead of.

4.1  Evaluation Metrics
To evaluate the performance of the proposed directional prediction
algorithm, we consider three key metrics: Mean Absolute Error
(MAE), Accuracy, and Average Response Time.
4.1.1  Mean Absolute Error (MAE). MAE measures the average
deviation between the predicted navigational direction and the
ground truth, both expressed in clock-hour format. The ground
truth directions were manually annotated separately for 300 images
from the NYUDepth V2 [11] dataset (indoor scenes) and 300 images
from the DIODE [19] dataset (outdoor scenes). For example, if the
◦

4.1.3  Average Response Time. The average response time refers to
the mean computational time (in milliseconds) taken by the algo-
rithm to generate the predicted direction for each input image. This
metric reflects the system’s suitability for real-time applications.
Among the metrics, MAE is the most crucial as it directly quan-
tifies angular deviation from the ground truth and thus determines
the reliability of navigation. While high Accuracy ensures con-
sistency, a low MAE guarantees that even near-misses are within
acceptable tolerance levels. Response Time, though secondary,
is significant for real-time deployment, particularly in assistive
navigation applications for visually impaired users.

To assess the usability of PathFinder and the Topline System, we
conducted a study with 15 blind or visually impaired (BLV) par-
ticipants from diverse age groups, education levels, mobility aids,
and geographical locations. The study sought to answer critical
questions such as:
• How well does the established system meet the demands
of BLV individuals, and how do they rate its effectiveness?
• How do alternative navigation schemes perform in terms
of usability and accuracy?
• What were the biggest issues that participants encountered
when using the app?
• What improvements or feature requests did the participants
make?
Participants were chosen based on various blindness character-
istics, such as congenital blindness, visual loss caused by medical
disorders (diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma, cataract, etc.), and acci-
dents. The sample also varied in age, education level, mobility aids,
and technology knowledge, allowing for a more thorough review
of the system. Table 7 provides detailed participant information,
including their blindness profile, approach procedures, ease of use
ratings, response times, and method rankings. Additionally, partic-
ipants provided feedback on indoor (7 participants) and outdoor
(8 participants) navigation experiences, using a five-point Likert

5.2  Participant EUngagement and Feedback
Collection
To ensure a diverse evaluation context, we implemented various
engagement strategies during the usability study of our application.
Some engaged in conversations with hospital patients, shopkeepers,
neighbors, and community members, while others requested feed-
back from teachers, friends, and family members. This approach
provided insights into real-world navigation challenges and user
preferences across different social settings. All participants were
informed of the purpose of the testing and provided their consent
to participate, ensuring that they understood their role in the eval-
uation process and the significance of their feedback.

Participants rated the ease of use of Approach 6 on a qualitative
scale (Good, Average, Bad):

The response time for participants to complete navigation tasks
ranged from 2 to 6 minutes, with an average of approximately 3.8
minutes. Participants using haptic feedback devices or GPS-based
smart assistants generally took longer, whereas those using white
canes and guide dogs exhibited more varied response times.
5.4  Participant Feedback on Indoor and
Outdoor Navigation
Participants evaluated their navigation experience in indoor (7
participants) and outdoor (8 participants) settings using a five-
point Likert scale, where 1 = VeryDifficult and 5 = Very Easy. The
responses were visualized through boxplots in figure 10, illustrating
percentile distributions.
5.4.1  Indoor Navigation.

Participants generally found indoor navi-
gation more challenging, citing difficulties in identifying obstacles,
turns, and structural variations.
5.4.2  Outdoor Navigation. Outdoor navigation received higher
Likert ratings, as participants benefited from open spaces, famil-
iar routes, and natural audio cues. However, a few participants
rated outdoor navigation due to traffic noise, moving obstacles, and
surface irregularities.

5.5  User Preferences for Navigation Schemes
To determine which navigation strategy felt most intuitive and prac-
tical for BLV users, participants ranked three distinct approaches:
Left/Forward/Right (DOF 3), Clock Hour Hand (DOF 13), and
In Degree (DOF 180). Participants rated these systems based on
their ease of use, accuracy, and real-world application. Ranking
results are summarized as follows:
• Clock Hour Hand scheme was selected as the first choice
by 7 out of 15 participants, making it the most preferred
option. Users praised its systematic approach to naviga-
tion, claiming that it closely corresponds with how they
currently understand directions.  o
• Left/Forward/Right was ranked first by six participants,
particularly those with minimal formal education orwlim-
ited technological knowledge. They found it more intuitive
d
since it resembled how a sighted guide would advise them.

WORK
While our suggested navigation system provides an efficient and
cost-effective solution for blind and low-vision (BLV) users, some
limitations must be addressed in future study.
One significant restriction is that our system does not direct peo-
ple to their eventual destination, but rather offers a short, obstacle-
free path in their immediate surroundings. While this enables
safe navigation in local surroundings, incorporating a global path-
planningmechanismmay improve long-distance mobility and route
optimization.
Another limitation is working in low-light circumstances, as the
system is based on image-based depth estimate. Depth accuracy
diminishes in dark situations, making obstacle detection less ac-
curate. Future enhancements could include the use of infrared or
LiDAR-based sensors to increase performance in low-light condi-
tions.
Our technique doesn’t struggle as much in dynamic situations,
where moving objects like pedestrians and automobiles present un-
foreseen challenges. Currently, the technology is intended for static
scenarios, which limits its real-time adaptability. However, using
haptic feedback instead of voice feedback might help resolve this
issue, enabling more dynamic and responsive interaction. Future
editions could investigate the use of real-time object tracking and
motion prediction algorithms to deal with dynamic impediments
more efficiently.

This study presented a novel, computationally efficient navigation
system designed to enhance mobility for Blind and Low Vision
(BLV) individuals. By leveraging a depth-based pathfinding algo-
rithm with a Depth-First Search (DFS) approach, we demonstrated
a low-cost, real-time solution that accurately detects free paths in

both indoor and outdoor environments. Comparative evaluations
showed that our proposed system offers a favorable trade-off be-
tween accuracy and computational efficiency, making it a viable
alternative to existing AI-powered navigation solutions that require
high-end hardware.
Our usability study, conducted with BLV participants, high-
lighted the system’s effectiveness in real-world settings, with the
majority of users preferring our approach for its intuitive directional
guidance and low inference time. While outdoor navigation yielded
higher satisfaction due to fewer obstacles and broader spaces, in-
door navigation posed challenges that require further refinement.o
Despite the promising results, our study identified several limi-
w
tations, including challenges in low-light environments, dynamic
obstacle handling, and long-distance navigation. Future research
